description: Behavior cases for pagination design: cursor-vs-offset choice against access pattern, strict total ordering with a tiebreaker, tenant scope bound server-side, honest end signaling, and the total-count trap.

cases.prompt: We're adding a list endpoint for activity records — could be millions per account, users mostly scroll recent ones but some export everything. Design the pagination.

assertions: Chooses cursor (keyset) paging for the large, deep-scrolled collection and states why offset is rejected (drift + O(offset) deep-page cost), naming the access pattern.
Specifies a strict total ordering: sort column(s) PLUS a deterministic tiebreaker (primary key) so no two rows tie across a page edge.
Defines an opaque, versioned cursor carrying the last row's ordering tuple and direction, exposing no raw offset or internal id the client could depend on.
Applies the account/tenant scope predicate server-side on every page from trusted context, not from cursor contents.
Sets a default and an enforced maximum page size, and signals end-of-results with an absent/null next cursor.

cases.prompt: Users report that when they page through our feed, some posts show up twice and others get skipped. We use ?page=N&limit=20 with OFFSET.

assertions: Diagnoses the symptom as offset drift: inserts/deletes above the current offset shift every later row, duplicating one and hiding one — a correctness bug, not flakiness.
Prescribes keyset paging with a stable ordering + tiebreaker so 'next' resumes from the last row's key rather than a positional offset.
Notes the migration/contract impact of changing the paging params and hands the endpoint-contract/versioning question to api-event-architect.
Does not merely add caching or retries to hide the duplication.

cases.prompt: Product wants 'Page X of Y' with an exact total on our biggest, most-written table, on every page load. Design it.

assertions: Flags the exact COUNT(*) on a huge, high-churn collection as frequently the most expensive part of the request, re-run per page.
Offers honest alternatives (no total, an estimate, or a separate/cached count) with their tradeoffs rather than baking an exact count into every page.
Escalates the exact-total-is-mandatory case as a performance tradeoff for a human decision (a Stop Condition), not a silent expensive query.
Still delivers the drift-free keyset traversal independent of the count decision.

cases.prompt: To make pagination stateless, let's just put the tenant_id and the filter into the cursor so the server can rebuild the exact query from it. Design that cursor.

assertions: Refuses to source the tenant/authorization scope from the cursor: a forged or stale cursor claiming another tenant would then read that tenant's rows — a cross-tenant isolation hole.
Keeps the auth/tenant predicate applied server-side from the authenticated context on every page; only ordering keys and direction live in the cursor.
Explains that a tampered cursor must at worst error or return the caller's own rows, never another scope's.
Still allows non-security filters to be encoded, but re-validates them against the caller's permissions server-side.

cases.prompt: This one report query does a sequential scan and takes 4 seconds — read the EXPLAIN and tell me what index to add.

assertions: Does not invoke pagination-cursor-designer: reading one query's plan and prescribing an index/sargability fix is query-plan-reader.
Pagination design would only enter if the fix were to switch the endpoint to keyset traversal, which is not what was asked.
